content:
- Ramki Gaddipati Office Hours Digital Twin Structured Summary

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- The meeting began with logistical clarifications and quickly moved into high-priority engineering discussions around partition ledgers. Participants walked through current feature limitations with an emphasis on aggregation, materialization, and support for limit codes and transaction policies. Ramki directed attention to defining the guiding principles and enforcing persona-driven design, urging teams to see all journeys from the end-user’s perspective.

- The conversation shifted to organization and prioritization of technical capabilities—debating whether to default-enable partition ledgers or defer until foundational journeys and basics (like system ledger creation and chart of accounts clarity) are in place. Ramki strongly advocated for clear, user/persona-oriented journeys before expanding capabilities.

- A significant portion of the meeting addressed data security, especially around highly sensitive card data. With a near-manifesto level of conviction, Ramki pushed for a “zero PII” architecture, advocating for irreversible tokenization, role-segregated cryptographic controls, and moving away from storing or exposing sensitive information—framing this as both a principle and a roadmap that should guide all future engineering.

- Finally, they discussed refining Customer Service Namespaces (CSNs) to better represent real customer journeys and proper SaaS boundaries, ensuring internal monitoring and productization reflect true service offerings, not just technical constructs.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Partition Ledger Scope: Aggregation Limitations Reasserted
- All system-time aggregations (balances, limits, postings) are unsupported in partitioned ledgers to simplify design and avoid inconsistencies.

- Materialization Policy: Enforcement at Individual Partition Only
- Ledger-level policies and balance rules will be enforced at the partition level only; no aggregate enforcement at the broader ledger level.

- User Journey-Centric Design Mandate
- Ramki directed the team to shift all future discussions and implementations to start from actual user journeys and personas, emphasizing flow from the end-user’s context.

- Deferral of Partition Ledger Default Enablement
- The team decided to defer automatically creating system ledgers as partitioned ledgers until more foundational journeys and onboarding flows are robust.

- Zero Identifiable Data Principle Established
- Ramki established a hard “zero PII” standard, requiring that no correlatable or joinable PII exist in any stored data—setting a new minimum bar for security posture.

- Tokenization Library and Detokenization Control Clarified
- Maintaining separation of roles: Tokenization/detokenization will always be mediated by a provided library or service. No direct cryptographic secrets or direct database lookups permitted outside Acropolis context.

- CSN Definitions Must Map to Real Customer Journeys
- Customer Service Namespaces are to be redefined to map “exactly” to the SaaS product’s boundaries and customer-facing journeys.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. Guiding Principle for Partition Ledgers:
- Ramki emphasized that the root principle for partition ledgers is the denial of all system-time aggregations. He discouraged enumerating downstream limitations individually—preferring clear, principled communication: “system-time aggregations are not supported, and all effects follow from this.”

2. Limit Code and Balance Support:
- He insisted supporting limit codes as policies is less complex than aggregation—and urged teams to implement limit code support, but clarified that lack of current implementation is about delivery priority, not technical impossibility. He asked for laziness/expediency to be called out distinctly from true blockers.

3. Persona-Centric Product Architecture:
- Ramki was unequivocal that future product discussions and engineering flows must start with user personas and journeys rather than technical constructs. He expressed frustration at abstract/technical framing, urging clarity around who the user is, where their journey begins, and what their objectives are (“no more journeys in English and system-level abstractions”).

4. Security as Architectural Tenet:
- Ramki articulated a “zero PII” philosophy with conviction, pushing beyond compliance to demand unbreakable irreversibility in persisted data. He laid out the principle that no stored data should be joinable or relatable to outside data sets, not merely encrypted, and proposed a future state where databases could be public without PII risk.

5. Separation of Product/Service Concerns:
- He delineated between internal engineering ownership (such as system ledgers in COA) and the broader application need, advocating for making policy providers “injectable” concerns—architecture should allow for service modularity, not tightly coupled dependencies.

6. CSN (Customer Service Namespace) Reframing:
- Ramki demanded that CSNs be mapped based on exposed SaaS boundaries and real customer contracts, and not as a reflection of clusters or technical artifacts. Observability and SLOs should be mapped to customer-facing contracts, so every SaaS product can independently articulate its provided services and success.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Partition Ledger Aggregation Principle Document
- Task: Update all technical documentation and developer onboarding materials to communicate that partition ledgers do not support system-time aggregation; enumerate all affected areas via that principle.
- Assigned To: Vijay Budhani, Pankaj Sonaiya (ledger engineering)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: State the principle up front; avoid listing all secondary effects unless tracing impact via that principle. Documentation must help all teams understand from first principles.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: None explicit, but urgency implied as it is required for immediate engineering clarity.

2. Limit Codes Support Implementation Review
- Task: Explore and, if feasible, implement limit code support in partition ledgers for policy enforcement at a per-transaction level.
- Assigned To: Vijay Budhani (lead), Amit Goyal (assessment/input)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Acknowledge ease of implementation vs. aggregation, prioritize based on operational need, and highlight any non-implementation as delivery trade-off—not inherent impossibility.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: None specified—should be reviewed in the near term and not blocked on aggregation features.

3. User Journey Mapping for System Ledger Creation
- Task: Define/document clear user journeys and entry points for system ledger creation, with personas and context (e.g., control center, finance center flows).
- Assigned To: Pankaj Sonaiya (journey owner), Vijay Budhani (collaborator)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Document specifics of initiation (where, by whom), expected user outcomes, and make journeys replicable. All journeys must be rooted in user perspective, not solely technical or admin flows.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: No explicit deadline, but strong urgency.

4. Zero PII Data Review and Roadmap
- Task: Inventory all storage, applications, and pipelines persisting PII/card data; prepare a phased roadmap to achieve “zero joinable/correlatable” PII (tokenization, detokenization delegation, and key management protocols).
- Assigned To: Praveen Darshanam (coordination/lead), Deep Jain, Abhilash Rao (domain leads)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Follow the principle that nothing should be joinable externally; centralize tokenization/detokenization in library/service, keep cryptographic secrets tightly controlled, and plan for architectural changes (e.g., confidential computing, HSM, key vault stratification).
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Long-term (“roadmap”—implied to start now with ongoing check-ins).

5. Redefine CSNs Based on Real SaaS Journeys
- Task: Redefine Customer Service Namespaces so each SaaS product’s boundary and customer-facing service is explicit and observable; involve product management to avoid “engineer-only” definition.
- Assigned To: Vivek Mantri (lead), Abhilash Rao (collaborator), Product Management (stakeholder)
- Ramki's Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Focus on customer journey, map every ingress flow of the SaaS boundary, distinguish cluster health from journey SLOs, include multiple customer levels if needed, and ensure clarity for SRE/CC teams.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Should initiate immediately and sync as per product organization cadence.

- VII. Jira Ticket

Scope:
- Partition Ledger Documentation Update
- User Journey Mapping for System Ledger Creation
- PII Data Review and Zero PII Roadmap (Phase 1: Discovery/Assessment)
- CSN Redefinition for SaaS Boundaries

Assignee:
- Partition Ledger Documentation: Vijay Budhani
- User Journey Mapping: Pankaj Sonaiya
- Zero PII Assessment: Praveen Darshanam
- CSN Redefinition: Vivek Mantri

Justification:
- These tickets encapsulate high-impact, foundational changes requiring formal tracking due to their cross-functional impact, architectural implications, and ongoing prioritization needs. Some actions (like cryptographic POC or immediate tactical patching) are too exploratory or pending product clarity for tracking at this stage. All recommended Jira ticket candidates are critical for future compliance, user experience, and system security.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- Partition Ledger Limitations: Ensure the principle (“no system-time aggregation”) is clearly surfaced at every onboarding and product documentation point. Consider quick all-team workshops to clarify downstream effects and eliminate confusion.
- Persona-Driven Journey Design: Regularly review if user journeys are kept up to date and get validated with product and operations teams, not only engineering.
- Zero PII Implementation: Prioritize a workshop to share the “zero PII” principle across product, engineering, and security teams, and schedule regular check-ins to track progress and blockers.
- Tokenization Library Upgrades: Coordinate cross-team validation sessions to ensure tokenization/detokenization libraries cover all use cases (API, library, future confidential computing support).
- CSN Progress: Involve product and ops stakeholders early, and routinely validate new/updated CSNs against real-life escalations or incident retrospectives for quality assurance.

metadata.partition_ledgers: feature limitations, migration, aggregation policies, and mutability
metadata.system_ledgers: creation flows, persona-driven journeys, and prioritization
metadata.customer_service_namespaces_(csns): mapping, SaaS product boundaries, and customer journey focus

title: 2024-10-16_Office Hours (17)
